Musical artist

**Edgar Holland Winter**[[3]](#cite_note-Powerline-3) (born December 28, 1946)[[4]](#cite_note-Madhouse-4) is an American multi-instrumentalist,[[5]](#cite_note-Hawaii-5) working as a vocalist along with playing keyboards, saxophone, and percussion.[[6]](#cite_note-Louder-6) His success peaked in the 1970s with his band the Edgar Winter Group and their popular songs "[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein_(instrumental) "Frankenstein (instrumental)")" and "[Free Ride](/wiki/Free_Ride_(song) "Free Ride (song)")" which remain staple tracks of classic rock radio.[[7]](#cite_note-GL-7)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He is the brother of blues singer and guitarist [Johnny Winter](/wiki/Johnny_Winter "Johnny Winter"), who died in 2014.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edgar_Winter&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Winter was born to John Winter II and Edwina Winter on December 28, 1946, in [Beaumont, Texas](/wiki/Beaumont,_Texas "Beaumont, Texas").[[8]](#cite_note-LarkinBlues-8) Both he and his elder brother [Johnny](/wiki/Johnny_Winter "Johnny Winter") were born with [albinism](/wiki/Albinism_in_humans "Albinism in humans").[[9]](#cite_note-LATimesJWObit-9) By the time he left the family home, Winter had mastered numerous instruments and reading and writing music.[[10]](#cite_note-Official-10)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edgar_Winter&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Edgar_Winter_Group_with_Rick_Derringer_1975.JPG/250px-Edgar_Winter_Group_with_Rick_Derringer_1975.JPG)](/wiki/File:Edgar_Winter_Group_with_Rick_Derringer_1975.JPG)

The Edgar Winter Group, Oct.1, 1975- L to R: [Chuck Ruff](/wiki/Chuck_Ruff_(musician) "Chuck Ruff (musician)"), [Rick Derringer](/wiki/Rick_Derringer "Rick Derringer"), [Dan Hartman](/wiki/Dan_Hartman "Dan Hartman"), Edgar Winter.

Winter composed and performed songs of numerous [genres](/wiki/Genre "Genre"), including rock, jazz, blues, and pop.[[10]](#cite_note-Official-10) His critically acclaimed[[10]](#cite_note-Official-10) 1970 debut release, *[Entrance](/wiki/Entrance_(album) "Entrance (album)")*,[[8]](#cite_note-LarkinBlues-8) was first to demonstrate his unique style of genre-blending musicianship. His early recording of "[Tobacco Road](/wiki/Tobacco_Road_(song) "Tobacco Road (song)")" gained attention. Edgar followed *[Entrance](/wiki/Entrance_(album) "Entrance (album)")* with two hit albums backed by his group White Trash, a group originally composed of musicians from Texas and Louisiana. White Trash, with Winter and Jerry Lacroix both on lead vocals and sax,[[11]](#cite_note-DiscogsTrash-11) released the studio album *[Edgar Winter's White Trash](/wiki/Edgar_Winter%27s_White_Trash_(album) "Edgar Winter's White Trash (album)")*,[[12]](#cite_note-AtticRecords-12) in 1971. It reached No. 111 on the charts,[[13]](#cite_note-Billboard-13) and 1972's follow-up album (partially recorded at New York's Apollo Theater) *[Roadwork](/wiki/Roadwork_(album) "Roadwork (album)")* achieved gold status.[[8]](#cite_note-LarkinBlues-8)

In late 1972, Winter brought together [Dan Hartman](/wiki/Dan_Hartman "Dan Hartman"), [Ronnie Montrose](/wiki/Ronnie_Montrose "Ronnie Montrose") and [Chuck Ruff](/wiki/Chuck_Ruff_(musician) "Chuck Ruff (musician)") to form The Edgar Winter Group,[[8]](#cite_note-LarkinBlues-8) who created such hits as the number one "[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein_(instrumental) "Frankenstein (instrumental)")"[[14]](#cite_note-RollingStone-14) and "[Free Ride](/wiki/Free_Ride_(song) "Free Ride (song)")" (with lead vocals by its writer Hartman).[[15]](#cite_note-NaplesNews-15) Released in November 1972, *[They Only Come Out at Night](/wiki/They_Only_Come_Out_at_Night "They Only Come Out at Night")*,[[8]](#cite_note-LarkinBlues-8) peaked at the number 3 position on the [Billboard Hot 200](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_200 "Billboard Hot 200")[[13]](#cite_note-Billboard-13) and stayed on the charts for 80 weeks. It was certified gold in April 1973 by the [RIAA](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America"),[[16]](#cite_note-The_Book_of_Golden_Discs-16) and double platinum in November 1986.[[17]](#cite_note-AOTY-17)

Winter invented the keyboard body strap[[3]](#cite_note-Powerline-3) early in his career, an innovation that allows him the freedom to move around on stage during his multi-instrument high-energy performances.[[10]](#cite_note-Official-10)

After *They Only Come Out at Night*, Winter released *[Shock Treatment](/wiki/Shock_Treatment_(Edgar_Winter_album) "Shock Treatment (Edgar Winter album)")*, featuring guitarist [Rick Derringer](/wiki/Rick_Derringer "Rick Derringer") in place of [Ronnie Montrose](/wiki/Ronnie_Montrose "Ronnie Montrose").[[18]](#cite_note-Philharmonic-18) That album contained Winter's third and final Top 40 single "River's Risin'",[[13]](#cite_note-Billboard-13) again sung by Hartman.[[19]](#cite_note-WolfgangsRiver-19) Later albums included *[Jasmine Nightdreams](/wiki/Jasmine_Nightdreams "Jasmine Nightdreams")*, *The Edgar Winter Group with Rick Derringer*, *[Together](/wiki/Together_(Johnny_and_Edgar_Winter_album) "Together (Johnny and Edgar Winter album)")*, *Recycled*, a reunion with White Trash, *Standing on Rock*, *[Mission Earth](/wiki/Mission_Earth_(album) "Mission Earth (album)")*, *Live in Japan*, *Not a Kid Anymore*, *[The Real Deal](/wiki/The_Real_Deal_(Edgar_Winter_album) "The Real Deal (Edgar Winter album)")*, *Winter Blues* and *[Rebel Road](/wiki/Rebel_Road "Rebel Road").*[[20]](#cite_note-Discography-20)

Winter also kept busy doing session work, playing saxophone on [Meat Loaf](/wiki/Meat_Loaf "Meat Loaf")'s "All Revved Up with No Place to Go",[[21]](#cite_note-MeatloafLiner-21) Dan Hartman's solo hit "[Instant Replay](/wiki/Instant_Replay_(song) "Instant Replay (song)")",[[22]](#cite_note-2ndDiscReplay-22) [Tina Turner](/wiki/Tina_Turner "Tina Turner")'s "[Simply the Best](/wiki/The_Best_(song) "The Best (song)")"[[23]](#cite_note-TurnerBestDiscogs-23) and [David Lee Roth](/wiki/David_Lee_Roth "David Lee Roth")'s remake of "[Just a Gigolo](/wiki/Just_a_Gigolo_(song) "Just a Gigolo (song)")",[[24]](#cite_note-VanHalenNews-24) as well as appearing on material by [Rick Derringer](/wiki/Rick_Derringer "Rick Derringer"),[[25]](#cite_note-DerringerInterv-25) [Johnny Winter](/wiki/Johnny_Winter "Johnny Winter"),[[26]](#cite_note-Secondhand-26) [Ronnie Montrose](/wiki/Ronnie_Montrose "Ronnie Montrose"),[[27]](#cite_note-Montrose-27) [Todd Rundgren](/wiki/Todd_Rundgren "Todd Rundgren"),[[28]](#cite_note-DoorsTrib-28) [Michael McDonald](/wiki/Michael_McDonald_(musician) "Michael McDonald (musician)")[[29]](#cite_note-DiscogsMcDonald-29) and many others.

Major national television and radio campaigns have used Winter's music to advertise their products.[[30]](#cite_note-IMDBEdgarOther-30) Winter has also made frequent television appearances, both to promote his music and to give his opinions, on shows such as *[Politically Incorrect](/wiki/Politically_Incorrect "Politically Incorrect")*.[[31]](#cite_note-CorbettInterview-31) Winter also starred with [George Hamilton](/wiki/George_Hamilton_(actor) "George Hamilton (actor)") in a commercial for [Miller Lite](/wiki/Miller_Lite "Miller Lite") beer.[[32]](#cite_note-VintageInterview-32) He appeared in the film *[Netherworld](/wiki/Netherworld_(film) "Netherworld (film)")*[[33]](#cite_note-IMDBMain-33) and the TV shows *The Cape*,[[10]](#cite_note-Official-10) *Mysterious Ways*,[[34]](#cite_note-AudioDB-34) *[Late Show with David Letterman](/wiki/Late_Show_with_David_Letterman "Late Show with David Letterman")*,[[35]](#cite_note-RockAtNight-35) and *[Jimmy Kimmel Live!](/wiki/Jimmy_Kimmel_Live! "Jimmy Kimmel Live!")*.[[36]](#cite_note-IMDBKimmel-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Edgar_Winter_%26_Ringo_Starr_20110624.jpg/250px-Edgar_Winter_%26_Ringo_Starr_20110624.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edgar_Winter_%26_Ringo_Starr_20110624.jpg)

Edgar Winter with Ringo Starr in 2011

Winter's music has been used in many film and television projects, including *[Netherworld](/wiki/Netherworld_(film) "Netherworld (film)")*, *[Air America](/wiki/Air_America_(film) "Air America (film)")*, *[Dazed and Confused](/wiki/Dazed_and_Confused_(film) "Dazed and Confused (film)")*, *[My Cousin Vinny](/wiki/My_Cousin_Vinny "My Cousin Vinny")*, *[Encino Man](/wiki/Encino_Man "Encino Man")*, *Son in Law*, *[What's Love Got to Do with It](/wiki/What%27s_Love_Got_to_Do_with_It_(1993_film) "What's Love Got to Do with It (1993 film)")*, *[Wayne's World 2](/wiki/Wayne%27s_World_2 "Wayne's World 2")*, *Starkid*, *[Wag the Dog](/wiki/Wag_the_Dog "Wag the Dog")*, *Knockabout Guys*, *Duets*, *Radio*, *[The Simpsons](/wiki/The_Simpsons "The Simpsons")*, *[Queer as Folk](/wiki/Queer_as_Folk_(2000_TV_series) "Queer as Folk (2000 TV series)")*, and *[Tupac: Resurrection](/wiki/Tupac:_Resurrection "Tupac: Resurrection")*.[[33]](#cite_note-IMDBMain-33) *[Tupac: Resurrection](/wiki/Tupac:_Resurrection "Tupac: Resurrection")*, a biography of rapper [Tupac Shakur](/wiki/Tupac_Shakur "Tupac Shakur"), produced and co-written by [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem"),[[37]](#cite_note-IMDBRessurection-37) [sampled](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") Winter's song "Dying to Live" as "[Runnin' (Dying to Live)](/wiki/Runnin%27_(Dying_to_Live) "Runnin' (Dying to Live)"),"[[38]](#cite_note-WhoSampledTupac-38) incorporating vocals by the [Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/Notorious_B.I.G. "Notorious B.I.G."), [Tupac](/wiki/Tupac_Shakur "Tupac Shakur"), and Winter himself. "Runnin'" peaked at number 5 on the Hot R&B/Hip Hop Singles Sales chart and the soundtrack was No. 1 for 8 consecutive weeks.[[39]](#cite_note-BillboardTupac-39) Blues performer [Jonny Lang](/wiki/Jonny_Lang "Jonny Lang") also covered "Dying to Live"[[40]](#cite_note-SecondLang-40) around this time. Winter performed the song solo at the piano on *Jimmy Kimmel Live!*[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Winter's 2003 CD and DVD titled *Live at the Galaxy* was recorded live at the Galaxy Theatre for Classic Pictures.[[41]](#cite_note-DiscogsGalaxy-41) It features the songs, "Keep Playing That Rock and Roll", "[Turn on Your Love Light](/wiki/Turn_On_Your_Love_Light "Turn On Your Love Light")", "[Free Ride](/wiki/Free_Ride_(song) "Free Ride (song)")", "Texas", "Show Your Love", "New Orleans", "[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein_(instrumental) "Frankenstein (instrumental)")" and "[Tobacco Road](/wiki/Tobacco_Road_(song) "Tobacco Road (song)")".[[42]](#cite_note-AllMusicGalaxy-42) In addition, the DVD includes a 30-minute documentary, *Edgar Winter: The Man and His Music*.[[18]](#cite_note-Philharmonic-18)

Winter also played with [Ringo Starr](/wiki/Ringo_Starr "Ringo Starr") in the ninth (2006), tenth (2008), eleventh (2010-2011) and fifteenth (2022-) iterations of his [All-Starr Band](/wiki/Ringo_Starr_%26_His_All-Starr_Band "Ringo Starr & His All-Starr Band").[[43]](#cite_note-AllStarHist-43) The band's 2010–11 tour featured the reunion of Winter and [Rick Derringer](/wiki/Rick_Derringer "Rick Derringer").[[44]](#cite_note-Bosso-44) From August to early-September 2017, The Edgar Winter Band toured as the opening act for both [Deep Purple](/wiki/Deep_Purple "Deep Purple") and [Alice Cooper](/wiki/Alice_Cooper "Alice Cooper") as they performed several North American tour dates as part of [The Long Goodbye Tour](/wiki/The_Long_Goodbye_Tour "The Long Goodbye Tour").[[45]](#cite_note-GlideTour-45)

In 2017, [Cherry Red](/wiki/Cherry_Red_Records "Cherry Red Records") released a remastered four-CD compilation of all of the Edgar Winter solo albums from 1970 until 1980, entitled *Tell Me in a Whisper: The Solo Albums 1970–1980*.[[46]](#cite_note-DiscogsSolo70-46) In 2018, Cherry Red also released a remastered six-CD compilation of Winter's White Trash band and the Edgar Winter Group band albums entitled *I've Got News for You: Featuring the Edgar Winter Group & Edgar Winter's White Trash 1971–1977*.[[47]](#cite_note-CherryRed-47)

### *Mission Earth*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edgar_Winter&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mission Earth")]

Winter also produced, arranged, and performed on the 1986 album *[Mission Earth](/wiki/Mission_Earth_(album) "Mission Earth (album)")*.[[48]](#cite_note-DiscogsEarth-48) This album's lyrics and music were written by [Scientology](/wiki/Scientology "Scientology") founder [L. Ron Hubbard](/wiki/L._Ron_Hubbard "L. Ron Hubbard").[[49]](#cite_note-AllMusicMission-49) Hubbard is said to have left detailed instructions and audio tapes for the musicians and producers to follow when making the album.[[50]](#cite_note-HubbardMusic-50) Winter described *Mission Earth* as "both a return to rock's primal roots and yet highly experimental." Winter wrote, "Ron's technical insight of the recording process was outstanding."[[51]](#cite_note-Bunker-51) Winter also described Hubbard's delineation of counter-rhythm in rock as something "which was nothing short of phenomenal, particularly in as much as it had then been entirely unexplored and only later heard in the African-based rhythms of [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon")'s work, some five years after Ron's analysis."[[34]](#cite_note-AudioDB-34)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edgar_Winter&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Winter and his wife, Monique,[[52]](#cite_note-Spectrum-52) live in [Beverly Hills, California](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_California "Beverly Hills, California").[[10]](#cite_note-Official-10) The couple have no children.[[4]](#cite_note-Madhouse-4) Winter stated in an interview: "I can see how that would be a wonderful rewarding thing, but I think there are enough people in the world" and that "it might have been more problematical if I had children with a career and all of it. I tour all the time. If I were to have children, I would want to be home all the time."[[53]](#cite_note-Flaska-53)
